summary: Joel highlights the longstanding frustration of trying to stop unwanted paper catalog deliveries, noting that catalog companies historically made it nearly impossible to opt out. He recommends Catalog Choice, a nonprofit service that contacts catalog merchants on your behalf to stop deliveries. He praises both the concept and the implementation of the service, noting its clean Ajax UI and graphic design.
key_points: Opting out of paper catalogs was historically futile — companies either couldn't remove you or would re-add you within months
Catalog Choice (a .org nonprofit) solves this by acting as an intermediary to contact merchants on your behalf
Joel praises the service for combining a great idea with excellent technical execution
money_quotes: Most of them had no way of doing that, and even if you got off the list, you'd always find your way back on again two months later.
Great idea, excellent implementation (very nice Ajax UI and great graphic design), and I'll let you know if it helps.
key_insight: The best solutions combine a genuinely useful idea with polished execution, as demonstrated by a nonprofit tackling the mundane but universal problem of unwanted catalogs.
